coredata.dc:title: FeWO4/g-C3N4 heterostructures decorated with N-doped graphene quantum dots prepared under various sonication conditions for efficient removal of noxious vapors
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2020 Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l.
coredata.dc:creator: Choi, Jong Uk
Jo, Wan-Kuen
coredata.dc:description: Herein, N-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) were prepared in an environmentally friendly manner using different sonication conditions by varying two important operating parameters: ultrasonic amplitude and sonication duration. In addition, they were decorated onto FeWO4/g-C3N4 heterostructures to afford a NGQD/FeWO4/g-C3N4 ternary-nanocomposites (NGQD/FWO/CN), which were tested for the photocatalytic removal of noxious vapors. The surface properties of the NGQDs varied with the sonication duration and the ultrasonic amplitude, and the photocatalytic capability of NGQD/FWO/CN varied with the type of decorated NGQDs, which were defined based on the sonication conditions. Moreover, the photocatalytic capability of FeWO4/g-C3N4 heterostructures decorated with sonication-treated NGQDs was higher than that of those decorated with NGQDs prepared without a sonication process. NGQD/FWO/CN also exhibited higher photocatalytic efficiency compared with selected photocatalyst counterparts, most likely due to the upconversion photoluminescence properties of NGQDs and the charge-separation abilities promoted by the electron-affinitive NGQDs and by the Z-scheme carrier-transport progress at the interfaces between the g-C3N4 and FeWO4. Specifically, the average removal efficiencies of vaporous 2-butoxy ethanol and ethyl benzene over a representative NGQD/FWO/CN sample under specified operational conditions were 97.3% (±0.7%) and 55.1% (±0.6%), respectively. Moreover, the pollutant removal capabilities of the photocatalysts were in accordance with their photocurrent measurements. The ternary nanocomposite also revealed excellent photocatalytic stability after successive uses for the decomposition of the model contaminants. Overall, this study highlights the promising role of nanocomposites decorated with sonication-processed NGQDs as photocatalysts for the efficient removal of noxious vapors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ultrasonic process
Ternary nanocomposite
Upconversion photoluminescence
Photocatalytic stability

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of NGQDs under different ultrasonic conditions
Measurements of hydroxyl radicals, photocurrents, and reactive species

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2020 Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l. All rights reserved.

item-info.ce:copyright: Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of experimental set-up for photocatalytic pollutant removal.
TEM images with size distribution (in inset) of NGQD-x samples prepared using different sonication durations: (a) NGQD-0, (b) NGQD-0.5, (c) NGQD-1.5, (d) NGQD-2.5, and (e) NGQD-3.5.
TEM images with size distribution (in inset) of NGQD-x samples prepared using different ultrasonic amplitudes: (a) NGQD-5.8, (b) NGQD-11.6, (c) NGQD-17.4, (d) NGQD-23.2, and (e) NGQD-29.0.
Upconverted PL spectra of NGQD-x samples prepared using different sonication durations: (a) NGQD-0, (b) NGQD-0.5, (c) NGQD-1.5, (d) NGQD-2.5, and (e) NGQD-3.5.
Upconverted PL spectra of NGQD-x samples prepared using different ultrasonic amplitudes: (a) NGQD-5.8, (b) NGQD-11.6, (c) NGQD-17.4, (d) NGQD-23.2, and (e) NGQD-29.0.
SEM images of FeWO4, g-C3N4, FeWO4/g-C3N4, and NGQD/FWO/CN-2.5.
TEM and HRTEM images with EDS elemental mapping of NGQD/FWO/CN-2.5.
XRD patterns of FeWO4, g-C3N4, FeWO4/g-C3N4, GQDs, and NGQD/FWO/CN-2.5.
XPS of NGQD/FWO/CN-2.5: (a) full-scan spectrum; (b) Fe 2p, (c) W 4f, (d) O 1s, (e) C 1s, and (f) N 1s.
UV–vis spectra of FeWO4, g-C3N4, FeWO4/g-C3N4, and NGQD/FWO/CN-2.5.
Efficiencies of the photocatalytic removal of 2BE and EBZ over FeWO4, g-C3N4, FeWO4/g-C3N4, and NGQD/FWO/CN-2.5 under daylight lamp irradiation.
Efficiencies of the photocatalytic removal of 2BE and EBZ over NGQD/FWO/CN-x samples prepared using different sonication durations.
Efficiencies of the photocatalytic removal of 2BE and EBZ over NGQD/FWO/CN-x samples prepared using different ultrasonic amplitudes.
Stability of NGQD/FWO/CN-2.5 for the photocatalytic 2BE and EBZ removal under daylight lamp irradiation.
Mechanisms of photocatalytic pollutant removal by NGQD/FWO/CN nanocomposites under daylight lamp irradiation.

head.ce:title: FeWO4/g-C3N4 heterostructures decorated with N-doped graphene quantum dots prepared under various sonication conditions for efficient removal of noxious vapors

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Environmental Engineering, Kyungpook National University, 80 University Road, Bukgu, Daegu, 702-701, South Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Environmental Engineering, Kyungpook National University, 80 University Road, Bukgu, Daegu, 702-701, South Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, N-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) were prepared in an environmentally friendly manner using different sonication conditions by varying two important operating parameters: ultrasonic amplitude and sonication duration. In addition, they were decorated onto FeWO4/g-C3N4 heterostructures to afford a NGQD/FeWO4/g-C3N4 ternary-nanocomposites (NGQD/FWO/CN), which were tested for the photocatalytic removal of noxious vapors. The surface properties of the NGQDs varied with the sonication duration and the ultrasonic amplitude, and the photocatalytic capability of NGQD/FWO/CN varied with the type of decorated NGQDs, which were defined based on the sonication conditions. Moreover, the photocatalytic capability of FeWO4/g-C3N4 heterostructures decorated with sonication-treated NGQDs was higher than that of those decorated with NGQDs prepared without a sonication process. NGQD/FWO/CN also exhibited higher photocatalytic efficiency compared with selected photocatalyst counterparts, most likely due to the upconversion photoluminescence properties of NGQDs and the charge-separation abilities promoted by the electron-affinitive NGQDs and by the Z-scheme carrier-transport progress at the interfaces between the g-C3N4 and FeWO4. Specifically, the average removal efficiencies of vaporous 2-butoxy ethanol and ethyl benzene over a representative NGQD/FWO/CN sample under specified operational conditions were 97.3% (±0.7%) and 55.1% (±0.6%), respectively. Moreover, the pollutant removal capabilities of the photocatalysts were in accordance with their photocurrent measurements. The ternary nanocomposite also revealed excellent photocatalytic stability after successive uses for the decomposition of the model contaminants. Overall, this study highlights the promising role of nanocomposites decorated with sonication-processed NGQDs as photocatalysts for the efficient removal of noxious vapors.

ce:section.ce:para: A class of carbon nitride, g-C3N4, is popularly employed as a prominent photocatalyst for environmental remediation, primarily owing to its outstanding chemical stability, high thermal endurance up to 600 °C, tunable electronic structure, and suitable flexibility [1,2]. However, plain g-C3N4 exhibits the fast recombining of charge carriers and is a medium-band-gap semiconductor; these traits adversely affect its photocatalytic capability under visible-light illumination [2–4]. This issue can be overcome by hybridizing g-C3N4 with other semiconductor photocatalysts with appropriate band alignments to create efficient semiconductor/semiconductor heterojunction photocatalysts [1–3]. Ferrous tungstate (FeWO4) is an especially attractive visible-light-responsive semiconductor for this purpose; FeWO4 exhibits the additional advantage of high oxidation capability owing to its high valence-band energy (+3.2 eV) [5,6].

In view of the prominent physicochemical characteristics of zero-dimensional (0D) graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [7–9], their decoration onto FeWO4/g-C3N4 heterostructures is presumed to result in better photocatalytic performance. In particular, GQDs exhibit a tunable electronic structure, high charge transfer rate, promoted light-harvesting capability, and distinct photocatalytic stability [7–9]. In addition, they are excellently dispersed in many aqueous and organic solvents owing to their amphipathic nature, which enables them to couple facilely with other materials to enhance catalytic performance [7].

GQD nanomaterials are generally prepared using chemical, electrochemical, or physical processes [10–14]. Among the aforementioned preparation methods, the physical, ultrasonic-shearing technique of graphene sheets is preferable because the use of ultrasound in the preparation of nanomaterials is both highly sustainable and efficient [15–17]. The application of ultrasound to solutions leads to the formation, growth, and implosion of bubbles through repeated compression and expansion processes [17,18]. Upon implosion of cavitation bubbles, thermodynamic hot spots are generated, and enormous amount of energy stored in the bubbles is released within an extremely short time. The released energy can be employed to synthesize various functional materials with novel properties under room-temperature conditions without extra heating and pressurization processes [15–17,19,20]. Therefore, the extreme conditions induced by the implosive collapse of cavitation bubbles enable the preparation of 0D GQDs from two-dimensional (2D) graphene sheets [9,12,14]. Notably, however, the aforementioned studies were undertaken under fixed sonication conditions, even though the physicochemical properties of GQDs can vary with sonication conditions such as the ultrasonic amplitude and the sonication duration [17]. This issue prompts an examination of the effect of sonication conditions on the physicochemical properties of GQDs during their preparation via the ultrasonic shearing technique.

Moreover, doping GQDs with nitrogen can promote their charge mobility and light-harvesting efficiencies, thereby expanding their photoresponse range [21–23]. Therefore, herein, nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) were prepared in an environmentally friendly manner using different sonication conditions by varying two important operating parameters: ultrasonic amplitude and sonication duration. In addition, they were decorated onto FeWO4/g-C3N4 heterostructures to afford NGQD/FeWO4/g-C3N4 ternary-nanocomposites (NGQD/FWO/CN), which were tested for the photocatalytic removal of vaporous 2-butoxy ethanol (2BE) and ethylbenzene (EBZ). These vapors were selected based on their frequent occurrence in ambient air and their detrimental effect on human health [24,25]. Major indoor sources of the model pollutants include building materials and household products [25]. Three catalyst counterparts (FeWO4, g-C3N4, and FeWO4/g-C3N4) were also evaluated for photocatalytic vapor removal. In addition, the photostability of NGQD/FWO/CN was evaluated on the basis of its successive utilization for photocatalytic vapor removal. Finally, a Z-scheme mechanism for NGQD/FWO/CN-catalyzed pollutant removal was proposed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of NGQDs under different ultrasonic conditions
ce:section.ce:para: NGQDs were prepared using an intermolecular fusion method, followed by sonication and hydrothermal processes. Typically, pyrene (3 g) was dispersed in HNO3 (69%, 240 mL). This dispersion was heated to 80 °C in a silicon oil bath, refluxed, and stirred for 12 h to afford trinitropyrene. After cooling, the dispersion was diluted in deionized water (3 L) and filtered to obtain a solid product. Subsequently, the solid material was washed with water and dried at 80 °C for 12 h to give yellow trinitropyrene powder. This powder (0.75 g) was then delivered to a hydrazine hydrate solution (0.2 M, 60 mL) and ultrasonicated under various amplitudes (5.8, 11.6, 17.4, 23.2, or 29.0 μm) and treatment times (0, 0.5, 1.5, 2.5, or 3.5 h); when the amplitude was varied, the treatment time was fixed at 2.5 h, and when the treatment time was varied, the amplitude was fixed at 17.4 μm. The ultrasonicated suspension was hydrothermally treated at 160 °C for 12 h, and this mixture was filtered to afford reddish-brown NGQD filtrates. The NGQD samples synthesized using sonication times of 0, 0.5, 1.5, 2.5, and 3.5 h were denoted as NGQD-0, NGQD-0.5, NGQD-1.5, NGQD-2.5, and NGQD-3.5, respectively. In addition, those synthesized using amplitudes of 5.8, 11.6, 17.4, 23.2, and 29.0 μm were denoted as NGQD-5.8, NGQD-11.6, NGQD-17.4, NGQD-23.2, and NGQD-29.0, respectively.
A detailed procedure for the g-C3N4 synthesis is listed in the Supplementary Material.

FeWO4 was fabricated via a hydrothermal process. In brief, FeCl3·6H2O (1.62 g) and Na2WO4·2H2O (1.98 g) were dispersed in deionized water (80 mL); this dispersion was stirred for 2 h. The resultant dispersion was then delivered to an autoclave, which was heated to 170 °C for 10 h. After cooling and filtration, the precipitate was cleaned using water and ethanol and dried at 110 °C for 15 h to afford a reddish-brown FeWO4 powder.
FeWO4/g-C3N4 was fabricated via a hydrothermal process. Typically, g-C3N4

(0.8 g) was dispersed in deionized water (80 mL) and the resultant dispersion was subjected to sonication for 2 h. Subsequently, FeCl3·6H2O (0.18 g) and Na2WO4·2H2O (0.22 g) were added to the aforementioned solution and the resultant mixture was stirred for 2 h. The mixture was then hydrothermally treated at 170 °C for 10 h. After the mixture was cooled and filtered, the obtained precipitate was rinsed with water and ethanol and dried at 110 °C for 15 h to afford a FeWO4/g-C3N4 powder.

The NGQD/FWO/CN nanocomposites were synthesized by hybridizing NGQDs with FeWO4/g-C3N4 (Scheme S1). Briefly, the as-fabricated FeWO4/g-C3N4 powder (1.0 g) was suspended in ethanol (40 mL), and this suspension was stirred for 0.5 h. The NGQD solution (2.7 mL) was added to the aforementioned suspension, and the resultant mixture was stirred for 12 h. The product was dried at 80 for 6 h in a vacuum oven to afford NGQD/FWO/CN powder. NGQD/FWO/CN heterostructures fabricated using NGQDs synthesized under sonication times of 0, 0.5, 1.5, 2.5, and 3.5 h were denoted as NGQD/FWO/CN-0, NGQD/FWO/CN-0.5, NGQD/FWO/CN-1.5, NGQD/FWO/CN-2.5, and NGQD/FWO/CN-3.5, respectively. In addition, those synthesized under the amplitudes of 5.8, 11.6, 17.4, 23.2, and 29.0 μm were denoted as NGQD/FWO/CN-5.8, NGQD/FWO/CN-11.6, NGQD/FWO/CN-17.4, NGQD/FWO/CN-23.2, and NGQD/FWO/CN-29.0, respectively.

The constructed samples were characterized using X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis, scanning electron microscopy (SEM), high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), UV–visible spectroscopy (UV–vis), and photoluminescence (PL) emission spectroscopy. Details of the sample characterization procedures are described in the Supplementary Material.

The efficiencies of the prepared samples in the photocatalytic decomposition of 2BE and EBZ were assessed using the experimental setup presented in Fig. 1 . A compressed-air cylinder containing highly-purified air (99.9999%) provided two dried air streams: one was delivered to a flash evaporation unit (0.5 L) for mixing with standard compounds, and the other was directed to water-containing impingers for water saturation. The two air streams were mixed in a buffering chamber (1.0 L), which served a secondary function of minimizing the variation in pollutant concentration. The resultant air mixture was subsequently allowed to flow into a Pyrex tube whose inside wall was coated with a thin catalyst film. The reactor coating method and photocatalytic operating conditions are described in detail in the Supplementary Material.

Before carrying out the photocatalytic pollutant removal tests, the experimental unit was cleaned by allowing ultra-pure air to pass through it overnight. The pollutant removal test was initiated by infusing standard gas into the reactor in the dark. After an adsorption equilibration of pollutants was observed, the lamp was turned on to start the pollutant decomposition. Additionally, a control test for the pollutant decomposition was undertaken using an empty reactor illuminated by a light source. The sampling and analytical procedures for gas pollutants are described in detail in the Supplementary Material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurements of hydroxyl radicals, photocurrents, and reactive species
ce:section.ce:para: Details for measuring the OH• population, photocurrent signals, and reactive species are described in the Supplementary Material.
The morphological properties and size distribution of the NGQD samples constructed under different sonication conditions were investigated by TEM analysis.

The TEM images of NGQDs exhibited quasi-spherical morphologies with different lateral sizes that depended on the sonication conditions (Figs. 2 and 3
). The average lateral sizes of NGQDs prepared using different sonication durations decreased in the order NGOD-0 (2.97 ± 0.06 nm) > NGQD-0.5 (2.87 ± 0.05 nm) > NGQD-1.5 (2.81 ± 0.06 nm) > NGQD-3.5 (2.64 ± 0.06 nm) > NGQD-2.5 (2.57 ± 0.05 nm), whereas those prepared using different ultrasonic amplitudes decreased in the order NGOD-5.8 (2.92 ± 0.05 nm) > NGQD-29.0 (2.88 ± 0.07 nm) > NGQD-11.6 (2.83 ± 0.06 nm) > NGQD-23.2 (2.68 ± 0.05 nm) > NGQD-17.4 (2.57 ± 0.06 nm). These results indicate that the lateral size of the NGQDs decreased with increasing sonication duration from 0 to 2.5 h but increased when the sonication duration was increased to 3.5 h. In addition, the lateral size of NGQDs decreased with increasing ultrasonic amplitude from 5.8 to 17.4 μm but decreased when the ultrasonic amplitude was increased to 29.0 μm. Comparatively, other researchers estimated average lateral sizes of GQDs between 2.8 and ~5 nm [9,11,26]. Consequently, among the NGQDs prepared using different sonication durations NGQD-2.5 displayed the smallest diameter, whereas, among the NGQDs prepared using different ultrasonic amplitudes, NGQD-17.4 displayed the smallest diameter.

Figs. S1 and S2 display photographs of the NGQD-x samples prepared under different sonication durations and ultrasonic amplitudes (suspended in deionized water with and without irradiation by UV light at 365 nm). Irrespective of the sonication conditions, all of the NGQD samples revealed a pale blue color under illumination but pale brown colors under dark conditions; the various NGQD-x samples were not visually distinguishable from each other.

The physical properties of the NGQD nanomaterials were further explored via UV–vis and PL spectra of the NGQD-x suspended in deionized water. In Fig. S3, the UV–vis spectra of all the NGQDs displayed two characteristic peaks at 290 and 387 nm, consistent with the results of earlier studies [9,26]. However, the light-absorption intensities of NGQDs prepared using different sonication durations decreased in the order NGQD-2.5 > NGQD-3.5 > NGQD-1.5 > NGQD-0.5 > NGQD-0, whereas those of NGQDs prepared using different ultrasonic amplitudes decreased in the order NGQD-17.4 > NGQD-23.2 > NGQD-11.6 > NGQD-29.0 > NGQD-0. This pattern of light-absorption intensities of the NGQDs was attributable to their size distributions shown in Figs. 2 and 3, which show greater light absorption with decreasing particle size, in line with previously reported results [26].

The NGQD samples under illumination with light in the wavelength range 700–800 nm exhibited upconversion PL properties, likely due to the multiphoton-active progress [26], indicating that NGQDs decorated on FeWO4/g-C3N4 heterostructures can improve their light absorbance and thus their photocatalytic activities toward the removal of noxious vapors. Figs. 4 and 5
illustrate the upconverted PL spectra of NGQD-x samples prepared using different sonication durations and different ultrasonic amplitudes, respectively. For the NGQD-x samples prepared using different sonication durations, the wavelengths of the maximum peaks increased in the order NGQD-2.5 < NGQD-3.5< NGQD-1.5 < NGQD-0.5 < NGQD-0, whereas for the NGQD-x samples prepared using different ultrasonic amplitudes, they increased in the order NGQD-17.4 < NGQD-23.2 < NGQD-11.6 < NGQD-29.0 < NGQD-0. Along with the corresponding size distributions of NGQDs (Figs. 2 and 3), these findings suggest that the wavelengths of the PL maximum increased with the NGQD size, in accordance with previously reported results [26].

The crystalline properties of the FeWO4, g-C3N4, FeWO4/g-C3N4, GQDs, and the NGQD/FWO/CN-2.5 were examined by XRD analysis (Fig. 8 ). The pure g-C3N4 displayed two distinctive peaks at 12.8° and 27.4°, which were assigned to the (100) facet matching the in-planar packing and the (002) facet matching the inter-planar stacking of a graphitic structure, respectively (JCPDS No. 87–1526). Regarding FeWO4, the major reflection peaks were indexed to the monoclinic ferberite structure based on the standard card (JCPDF-0712390) [6]. The patterns of FeWO4/g-C3N4 and NGQD/FWO/CN-2.5 displayed defined diffraction peaks of g-C3N4 and FeWO4, indicative of the excellent incorporation of these compounds. Nevertheless, peaks associated with NGQDs were not observed in the XRD image of NGQD/FWO/CN-2.5, possibly due to the small amount of NGQDs loaded in this nanocomposite.

Fig. 9 illustrates the surface chemical composition and bonds of the NGQD/FWO/CN-2.5. The full-scan XPS spectrum of NGQD/FWO/CN-2.5 comprised characteristic peaks of Fe, W, O, C, and N (Fig. 9a). The Fe 2p spectrum includes five distinct peaks at 710.6, 713.5, 718.5, 724.0, and 732.3 eV, which were associated with Fe2+ 2p3/2, Fe3+ 2p3/2, a satellite Fe peak, Fe2+ 2p1/2, and Fe3+ 2p1/2, correspondingly (Fig. 9b). The W 4f peaks at 35.3 and 37.3 eV were indexed to W 4f7/2 and W 4f5/2 transitions, correspondingly (Fig. 9c) [28]. The O 1s spectrum comprised four deconvoluted peaks at 530.8, 531.7, 532.4, and 533.5 eV corresponding to OH bonds, metallic oxide Fe–O and W–O–W bonds, and H–O–H bonds, respectively (Fig. 9d) [6,28]. The C 1s spectrum featured three peaks at 284.4, 286.0, and 288.0 eV matching C–C bonds, N–C bonds, and C–N3 bonds (Fig. 9e) [6,29]. The N 1s spectrum displayed four distinct peaks at 398.4, 399.6, 400.8, and 404.3 eV corresponding to C–NC bonds, N–C3 bonds, C–N–H, and the charging effect of heterocycles, respectively (Fig. 9f) [6,29].

The light-absorption characteristics of FeWO4, g-C3N4, FeWO4/g-C3N4, and NGQD/FWO/CN-2.5 were explored by diffuse reflectance UV–vis spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 10 and Fig. S4, g-C3N4 and FeWO4 exhibited efficient visible-light absorption properties with band gaps of 2.74 and 2.24 eV, correspondingly; these values were similar to those previously reported [6,29]. FeWO4/g-C3N4 exhibited higher light-absorption efficiencies than primal g-C3N4, suggesting that the absorption spectrum of the latter was tuned by FeWO4. Furthermore, NGQD/FWO/CN-2.5 exhibited a higher light-harvesting capability than FeWO4/g-C3N4, probably owing to the upconverted illumination of NGQDs decorated onto the latter (Figs. 4 and 5). The band gaps of FeWO4/g-C3N4 and NGQD/FWO/CN-2.5 were 2.62 and 2.60 eV, respectively. Meanwhile, the highest light absorption efficiency of FeWO4 was ascribed to its excellent intrinsic optical properties [6].

The FeWO4/g-C3N4 heterostructures decorated with NGQDs prepared under various sonication conditions and the three catalyst counterparts (FeWO4, g-C3N4, and FeWO4/g-C3N4) were examined for the photocatalytic removal of noxious 2BE and EBZ. The control experiment undertaken in the absence of a catalyst indicated no appreciable decomposition of the selected pollutants under illumination. By contrast, in the presence of the as-prepared catalysts, distinct photocatalytic decomposition of the target pollutants was observed (Fig. 11 ). NGQD/FWO/CN samples displayed the greatest photocatalytic removal of both 2BE and EBZ. Specifically, the average removal efficiencies of 2BE and EBZ over NGQD/FWO/CN-2.5 were 97.3% (±0.7%) and 55.1% (±0.6%), respectively. By comparison, the average 2BE and EBZ removal efficiencies achieved with FeWO4/g-C3N4 were 56.7% (±0.9%) and 20.8% (±0.7%), respectively, whose achieved with g-C3N4 were 35.0% (±1.0%) and 15.4 (±0.5%), respectively, and those achieved with FeWO4 were 14.0% (±0.8%) and 4.1% (±0.9%), respectively. Moreover, the pollutant removal capabilities of the photocatalysts were in accordance with the results of their measured photocurrent, which decreased in the order NGQD/FWO/CN-2.5 > FeWO4/g-C3N4 > g-C3N4 > FeWO4 (Fig. S5). The greater photocatalytic capability of FeWO4/g-C3N4 compared with those of primal g-C3N4 and FeWO4 was ascribable to the Z-scheme carrier-transport progress at the interfaces between the two components in the nanocomposite (described in detail in Section 3.5). Moreover, the greater photocatalytic capability of NGQD/FWO/CN-2.5 compared with that of FeWO4/g-C3N4 was assigned to the promoted carrier-separation capability of the former as a result of the electron-affinitive properties of the NGQDs [7,26]. This assertion is supported by the lower PL emission intensity of NGQD/FWO/CN-2.5 than that of FeWO4/g-C3N4 (Fig. S6), because the lower PL emission intensity reflects the high charge-separation ability [13,30]. The greater photocatalytic capability of NGQD/FWO/CN-2.5 compared with that of FeWO4/g-C3N4 is also ascribable to the promoted optical properties of NGQD/FWO/CN-2.5, which exhibited a higher light absorption efficiency than the FeWO4/g-C3N4 (Fig. 10), due to the upconversion PL nature of NGQDs in the NGQD/FWO/CN-2.5 (Figs. 4 and 5).

Furthermore, to explore the effect of the sonication conditions on the photocatalytic capability of the NGQD/FWO/CN nanocomposite, the photocatalytic efficiencies of the FeWO4/g-C3N4 heterostructures decorated with NGQDs prepared under various sonication conditions were compared with each other regarding the removal of 2BE and EBZ. Fig. 12 illustrates the efficiencies of the photocatalytic removal of 2BE and EBZ over NGQD/FWO/CN samples prepared using different sonication durations. The photocatalytic capabilities of the NGQD/FWO/CN nanocomposites increased in the order NGQD/FWO/CN-0 < NGQD/FWO/CN-0.5 < NGQD/FWO/CN-1.5 < NGQD/FWO/CN-3.5 < NGQD/FWO/CN-2.5, indicating that the photocatalytic capability of the NGQD/FWO/CN increased as the sonication duration increased to 2.5 h but decreased afterward. These results also demonstrate that the sonication process in the synthesis of NGQDs improves the photocatalytic capability of NGQD/FWO/CN nanocomposites. In addition, Fig. 13 exhibits the efficiencies of photocatalytic pollutant removal over NGQD/FWO/CN samples prepared using different ultrasonic amplitudes. The photocatalytic capabilities of the NGQD/FWO/CN nanocomposites increased in the order NGQD/FWO/CN-5.8 < NGQD/FWO/CN-29.0 < NGQD/FWO/CN-11.6 < NGQD/FWO/CN-23.2 < NGQD/FWO/CN-17.4, indicating that the photocatalytic capability of NGQD/FWO/CN increased as the ultrasonic amplitude increased to 17.4 but decreased afterward. The pollutant removal capabilities of the FeWO4/g-C3N4 heterostructures decorated with NGQDs prepared under different sonication conditions were in accordance with their corresponding photocurrent signals (Figs. S6 and S7). The orders of the photocatalytic capabilities and photocurrent signals of the NGQD/FWO/CN nanocomposites were attributed to the upconverted PL properties of NGQDs contained in these nanocomposites because these orders are consistent with the reverse order of wavelengths of the upconverted PL maximum in NGQDs prepared under different sonication conditions (Figs. 4 and 5). The orders of the photocatalytic capabilities and photocurrent signals were also ascribable to the corresponding charge-separation abilities, which were demonstrated by the corresponding PL emission intensities of the NGQD/FWO/CN nanocomposites (Figs. S8 and S9).

The photocatalytic stability test of the NGQD/FWO/CN nanocomposite was undertaken to inspect its practical applicability for environmental remediation by utilizing it five consecutive times for the removal of 2BE and EBZ. Fig. 14 illustrates that the removal efficiencies of the target pollutants did not noticeably change during the consecutive runs, maintaining >97% of their initial removal efficiencies after the last run. Additionally, neither the photocurrent signals (Fig. S7) nor the XRD patterns (Fig. S8) of new and used NGQD/FWO/CN-2.5 displayed any noticeable change, confirming its photocatalytic stability. This stability was ascribed to the enhanced charge-separation capability of NGQD/FWO/CN, which was most likely due to the electron-affinitive feature of the NGQDs and to the Z-scheme reaction (described in detail in Section 3.5). Consequently, the NGQD/FWO/CN nanocomposite demonstrates impressive photocatalytic stability; accordingly, this material represents a promising photocatalyst for the removal of noxious vapors.

According to the electronic structures of g-C3N4 and FeWO4 contained in NGQD/FWO/CN and its promoted catalytic ability to control noxious vapors, we proposed a possible reaction mechanism (Fig. 15 ). According to their corresponding Tauc plots (Fig. S4), the FeWO4 and g-C3N4 exhibited band gaps of 2.24 and 2.74 eV; by contrast, their valence band (VB) values determined by XPS (Fig. S9) were +3.01 and + 1.74 eV, respectively. To assess the charge transfer process of NGQD/FWO/CN, hydroxyl radical (OH•) measurements were carried out using g-C3N4, FeWO4, FeWO4/g-C3N4, and NGQD/FWO/CN-2.5 (Fig. S10). For all of the investigated photocatalysts, OH• formed during the photocatalytic process and its concentration increased with increasing illumination time. Additionally, NGQD/FWO/CN-2.5 revealed the greatest OH• formation over the illumination period. The greater OH• concentration on NGQD/FWO/CN-2.5 catalyst than on the FeWO4 catalyst indicates that the carrier-transport route over the former does not correspond to the heterojunction type II process. In this process, the positive holes in the VB of FeWO4 could migrate to the VB of g-C3N4 because of the higher VB level of FeWO4 (+3.01 eV) relative to that of the g-C3N4 (+1.74 eV). Thus, the migrated holes would not form OH• because of the lower VB potential of the g-C3N4 VB compared with the hydroxyl ion (OH−)/OH• oxidation energy (+1.90 eV) [31].

As an alternative, the improved OH• formation on the NGQD/FWO/CN-2.5 surface was assigned to its Z-scheme charge-transfer progress. Upon irradiation, both g-C3N4 and FeWO4 in NGQD/FWO/CN-2.5 are excited to produce positive holes and electrons in the corresponding VB and conduction band (CB), accordingly. The hole in the FeWO4 VB oxidizes OH− to OH• because its VB edge energy (+3.01 eV) is greater than the OH−/OH• oxidation energy. This charge-transfer process explains the enhanced OH• formation on the NGQD/FWO/CN-2.5 surface, which was determined in the aforementioned OH• measurements. Synchronously, the electrons in the FeWO4 CB move to the g-C3N4 VB to couple with holes because of their energy discrepancy. The electrons remaining in the g-C3N4 CB further migrates to NGQDs because of their electron affinity, and then the moved electrons react with oxygen to form superoxide anion radicals (O2 •−) [9]. Moreover, NGQDs promote the light-harvesting properties of NGQD/FWO/CN owing to their upconversion PL properties, as shown in Figs. 4 and 5. Consequently, active OH• and O2 •− decompose noxious 2BE and EBZ into harmless carbon dioxide and water vapor, along with certain intermediates.

The Z-scheme carrier-transport routes in the NGQD/FWO/CN-2.5 surface were confirmed by examining the reactive species generated during the photocatalysis. The scavenger experiments revealed that O2 •–, h+, and OH• played important roles in the photocatalytic progress, with the highest contribution observed for O2 •– (Fig. S11). If the photocatalytic reaction follows the type II heterojunction mechanism, electrons probably migrate from the g-C3N4 CB (−1.0 eV) to the FeWO4 CB (+0.77 eV) because of the less positive value of the former. In this case, the transferred electron in the FeWO4 CB cannot generate O2 •– because the CB energy is more positive than the energy needed for the O2/O2 •– conversion (−0.046 eV) [30]. Therefore, these findings confirm that the photocatalytic progress over NGQD/FWO/CN corresponds to the Z-scheme mechanism.

This study set out to demonstrate that the sonication condition influences the surface properties of NGQDs and thus the photocatalytic capabilities of the NGQD-decorated FeWO4/g-C3N4 heterostructures. We achieved the objectives of this study by finding that the lateral size and the surface characteristic of NGQDs varied with the ultrasound amplitude and the sonication duration and that FeWO4/g-C3N4 heterostructures decorated with sonication-processed NGQDs under different conditions exhibited different photocatalytic capabilities for the removal of noxious vapors. Moreover, the abovementioned nanocomposites revealed prominent photocatalytic stability, demonstrating the feasibility of their prolonged use. The enhanced photocatalytic capability and stability of NGQD/FWO/CN were ascribable to the upconversion PL properties of NGQDs and/or the promotion of the charge-separation abilities by the electron-affinitive NGQDs and the Z-scheme carrier-transport progress at the interfaces between g-C3N4 and FeWO4. Therefore, these results validate that the nanocomposites decorated with sonication-processed NGQDs is a promising photocatalyst for the efficient treatment of noxious vapors.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The author declares that no conflicting actual or potential interests exist.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Korea government (MSIP) (No. 2017R1A4A1015628).
